candidate_problems.text: What is the non-arbitrary threshold at which a multilayer, self-correcting system genuinely has meaning rather than merely sophisticated classification or control? The conjecture says meaning 'begins' when syntax, statistics, memory, feedback, and cross-context constraint become tightly coupled, but it leaves open a principled criterion for this transition. Without such a criterion, the view risks vagueness about borderline cases such as current language models, animals, thermostats, or distributed institutions.

How can emergent semantic roles secure genuine aboutness or world-directed reference rather than only internally coherent functional organization? The conjecture appeals to prediction, error-correction, social correction, and success conditions, but it remains unresolved whether these system-level dynamics are sufficient to explain why an internal state is about a particular external feature instead of merely playing a useful role within the system. This is the central tension between internal constraint-based semantics and external reference.

What formal architecture or dynamical model can unify syntax, statistics, pattern recognition, memory, and recursive feedback into a testable theory of meaning? The conjecture is explanatorily attractive at a systems level, but it does not yet specify the relevant variables, feedback loops, invariants, or failure modes. A precise model is needed to distinguish this proposal from neighboring views, generate predictions, and identify which organizational features are necessary versus incidental for semantic emergence.
